methods.description: Process for preparing ice core samples for tephra analysis to determine their relation to volcanic eruptions.

steps.description: Archived ice from the interval between 78.515 and 78.655 m was sampled directly for tephra analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: sample ice core sections for tephra analysis

steps.description: The direct ice samples were transferred to individual Nalgene bottles and melted at room temperature.
steps.searchable_summary: melt ice samples in Nalgene bottles

steps.description: The meltwater was then centrifuged to concentrate any particulate material present.
The concentrated meltwater was evaporated onto frosted 28 × 48 mm microprobe slides and covered with Buehler EpoxyCure 2 resin.
steps.searchable_summary: evaporate and resin-coat particulates on microprobe slides

steps.description: The presence of glass tephra shards was assessed at 100-400× magnification using a polarising light microscope.

methods.description: Procedure for analyzing the geochemical composition of tephra shards to compare with known volcanic events.

steps.description: The epoxy mount was ground using 12 µm alumina powder and then polished using 6, 3 and 1 µm diamond paste.
steps.searchable_summary: grind and polish epoxy mount for analysis

steps.description: The surface of the resin and exposed shards was then carbon-coated prior to analysis.
Concentrations of 11 major and minor elements within individual glass shards were analyzed using a JEOL FEGSEM 6500F.
Geochemical data were normalized to an anhydrous basis using eight elements consistently analyzed in all studies.
steps.searchable_summary: normalize geochemical data to anhydrous basis

steps.description: Statistical comparisons were made using the statistical distance (D2) test and the similarity coefficient (SC) function.
steps.searchable_summary: perform statistical comparisons using D2 and SC tests
